Be prepare to have hypertension after it lol.", "date": "2018-01-02 21:35:31"}, {"review_id": "reviewid_1500", "user_id": "userid_1030", "business_ref": "businessref_37", "rating": "1", "useful": "3", "funny": "0", "cool": "0", "text": "Kelly is hands down the best orange theory trainer I have ever encountered. She is also the only reason I put up with the atrocious customer service at this location and unfair charges for so long. I have been having issues with the app not booking me in classes, showing me as not in classes I am booked in, and not cancelling classes I cancelled in time. This has resulted in several disputable charges on my account. Each time I called I was told there were no other reported issues with the app and I must be mistaken. And that if there was an issue with the app, they are just a franchise and the app is owned and ran by orange theory not the franchise location. This went on for months and to be perfectly honest I thought maybe I was mistaken. Last week I was booked in Kelly's 10 am Saturday class and I screenshotted it in the app as booked and screenshotted it in my calendar (as this is also done via the app). I was on the wait list and often check my wait list number throughout the week. On Friday when I went to see if I had been bumped up, I was no longer booked in the class. When I called to ask them about the app, she told me I was still on the wait list as number 2. At this point I flipped out because had I not called I would have just found another workout for that morning assuming I was again mistaken AGAIN and thus "no showed" thinking I wasn't booked in the class when I was. Thus, resulting in a charge for a class I "no showed", when in reality the app is not providing accurate info but I am making decisions as though it is. Thank god I screenshotted it or else they could have continued dodging me and saying I am mistaken. I asked Emily if I could please have a number to speak with someone at orange theory about the app issues. After being on hold for 10 minutes she came back and said that the manager would call me. It has been almost a week and no call. I am cancelling my membership today, but if you want good customer service, accountability, and you don't want to be robbed blind, I suggest you use a different orange theory location. I am genuinely upset about this because like I said, Kelly is worth the bullshit because she is such an amazing trainer. They are lucky to have her and most people probably put up with their bullshit because of her. I will probably pay the higher "drop in fee" to get my Kelly fix, but I won't let them have access to any of my cards via a membership. They will take your money with zero remorse as to whether you or their app caused the issue. function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Load all data
users_2016_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:20']
businesses_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:30']

with open(users_2016_file, 'r') as f:
    users_2016 = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

with open(businesses_file, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Extract user IDs for 2016
user_ids_2016 = set(user['user_id'] for user in users_2016)
print(f"Total users from 2016: {len(user_ids_2016)}")

# Filter reviews by these users
reviews_by_2016_users = [
    review for review in reviews 
    if review['user_id'] in user_ids_2016
]
print(f"Total reviews by 2016 users: {len(reviews_by_2016_users)}")

# Create business ID mapping (businessref_X -> businessid_X)
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']  # e.g., businessid_49
    # Extract the number part
    num = business_id.split('_')[1]
    business_ref = f"businessref_{num}"
    business_map[business_ref] = business

print(f"Total businesses: {len(business_map)}")

# Extract categories and count reviews
category_review_count = Counter()
review_per_business = {}

for review in reviews_by_2016_users:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if business_ref in business_map:
        business = business_map[business_ref]
        description = business.get('description', '')
        
        # Extract categories from description
        # Remove location prefix if present
        desc_clean = re.sub(r'^Located at [^,]+, [^,]+, [^,]+, ', '', description)
        
        # Extract categories (look for patterns like "in Category1, Category2, and Category3" or "in Category1, Category2, Category3")
        categories = []
        
        # Try different patterns
        patterns = [
            r'in ([^\.]+)',  # "in Category1, Category2, and Category3"
            r'including ([^\.]+)',  # "including Category1, Category2, and Category3"
            r'offers ([^\.]+)',  # "offers Category1, Category2, and Category3"
        ]
        
        for pattern in patterns:
            match = re.search(pattern, desc_clean)
            if match:
                cats_text = match.group(1)
                # Split by commas and "and"
                cats = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', cats_text)
                categories = [cat.strip() for cat in cats if cat.strip()]
                break
        
        # If no pattern matched, try splitting the whole description
        if not categories:
            # For descriptions like "Education, Elementary Schools, Child Care..."
            cats = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', desc_clean)
            categories = [cat.strip() for cat in cats if cat.strip()]
        
        # Count reviews per category
        for category in categories:
            category_review_count[category] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5_categories = category_review_count.most_common(5)

print("\nTop 5 business categories by total reviews from 2016 users:")
for i, (category, count) in enumerate(top_5_categories, 1):
    print(f"{i}. {category}: {count} reviews")

result = {
    "top_5_categories": [
        {"category": cat, "review_count": count}
        for cat, count in top_5_categories
    ],
    "total_reviews_analyzed": len(reviews_by_2016_users)
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

I've purchased about 10 used cars in the last 25 years. This has been the worst experience by far. Test drove and purchased a used car on 3/31 for my 16 year old daughter, the salesman knew it was for her. Bought the 3 year 'bumper to bumper' warranty the finance guy said was the same as if it \"just came off the showroom floor\". They needed to remove pin stripes and fix a tire pressure sensor so they agreed to deliver it; we live almost two hours away. Met them after dark near the highway on 4/2; the check engine light came on on our way home. Texted a pic to the salesman and called him the next day -- no response. Left several voice mails for the warranty/finance guy - no response. {"code": "import json
from collections import Counter
import re

# Load the data from stored files
print("Loading data from files...")

# Load 2016 users (var_functions.query_db:12)
print("Loading 2016 users...")
users_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(users_file, 'r') as f:
    users_2016 = json.load(f)
user_ids_2016 = set(user['user_id'] for user in users_2016)
print(f"Found {len(user_ids_2016)} users from 2016")

# Load all reviews (var_functions.query_db:36)
print("Loading all reviews...")
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    all_reviews = json.load(f)
print(f"Total reviews loaded: {len(all_reviews)}")

# Filter reviews by 2016 users
reviews_by_2016_users = [
    review for review in all_reviews 
    if review['user_id'] in user_ids_2016
]
print(f"Reviews by 2016 users: {len(reviews_by_2016_users)}")

# Create business reference map (businessref_X -> business object)
print("Loading businesses...")
businesses_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:30']
with open(businesses_file, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']  # e.g., businessid_49
    num = business_id.split('_')[1]
    business_ref = f"businessref_{num}"
    business_map[business_ref] = business

print(f"Mapped {len(business_map)} businesses")

# Process reviews and extract categories
category_counter = Counter()
unmatched_businesses = set()

print("Processing reviews and extracting categories...")
for review in reviews_by_2016_users:
    business_ref = review.get('business_ref')
    if not business_ref:
        continue
        
    if business_ref not in business_map:
        unmatched_businesses.add(business_ref)
        continue
    
    business = business_map[business_ref]
    description = business.get('description', '')
    
    # Extract categories from description
    categories = []
    desc_clean = re.sub(r'^Located at [^,]+, [^,]+, [^,]+, ', '', description)
    
    # Try to extract categories after keywords like "in", "including", "offers"
    patterns = [
        r'in ([^\.]+)',
        r'including ([^\.]+)',
        r'offers ([^\.]+)',
        r'provides ([^\.]+)',
        r'offering ([^\.]+)'
    ]
    
    found_categories = False
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc_clean)
        if match:
            cats_text = match.group(1)
            # Split by comma and "and"
            cats = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', cats_text)
            categories = [cat.strip() for cat in cats if cat.strip()]
            if categories:
                found_categories = True
                break
    
    # If no pattern matched, try the entire cleaned description
    if not found_categories:
        cats = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', desc_clean)
        categories = [cat.strip() for cat in cats if cat.strip()]
    
    # Count reviews for each category
    for category in categories:
        category_counter[category] += 1

print(f"Unmatched business references: {len(unmatched_businesses)}")

# Get top 5 categories
print("\nTop 5 categories by review count:")
top_5 = category_counter.most_common(10)  # Get more to see patterns
for i, (cat, count) in enumerate(top_5[:5], 1):
    print(f"{i}. {cat}: {count} reviews")

result = {
    "top_5_categories": [
        {"category": cat, "review_count": count}
        for cat, count in top_5[:5]
    ],
    "total_reviews_analyzed": len(reviews_by_2016_users),
    "total_categories_found": len(category_counter)
}

print("\n__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

print(f"Found {len(reviews_by_2016_users)} reviews by 2016 users")

    businesses = json.load(f)

# Create mapping from business_ref to business
business_map = {}
for b in businesses:

    num = business_id.split('_')[1]
    business_ref = f"businessref_{num}"
    business_map[business_ref] = b

print(f"Mapped {len(business_map)} businesses")

# Process categories
category_counter = Counter()
unmatched = 0

for review in reviews_by_2016_users:
    business_ref = review.get('business_ref')
    if not business_ref or business_ref not in business_map:
        unmatched += 1
        continue
    
    description = business_map[business_ref].get('description', '')
    
    # Clean description
    desc_clean = re.sub(r'^Located at [^,]+, [^,]+, [^,]+, ', '', description)
    desc_clean = re.sub(r'^Located at [^,]+, [^,]+, [^\.]+\.?\s*', '', desc_clean)
    
    # Extract categories
    categories = []
    patterns = [r'in ([^\.]+)', r'including ([^\.]+)', r'offers ([^\.]+)', r'category of [^']*'([^\']+)'[^']*']
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc_clean, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            cats_text = match.group(1)
            # Split by comma and "and"
            cats = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', cats_text)
            categories = [c.strip() for c in cats if c.strip()]
            break
    
    # Fallback: split by commas
    if not categories:
        cats = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', desc_clean)
        categories = [c.strip() for c in cats if c.strip() and len(c.strip()) < 100]
    
    # Count reviews per category
    for category in categories:
        category_counter[category] += 1

print(f"Unmatched business refs: {unmatched}")
print(f"Total unique categories: {len(category_counter)}")

# Get top 5
top_5 = category_counter.most_common(5)
print("\nTop 5 categories:")
for i, (cat, count) in enumerate(top_5, 1):
    print(f"{i}. {cat}: {count} reviews")

result = {
    "top_5_categories": [(cat, count) for cat, count in top_5],
    "total_reviews_analyzed": len(reviews_by_2016_users),
    "unmatched_businesses": unmatched
}

print("\n__RESULT__:")

The top 5 business categories that have received the most total reviews from these users are:

1. **Restaurants** - 22 reviews
2. **American (New)** - 16 reviews  
3. **Food** - 14 reviews
4. **Local Services** - 11 reviews
5. **American (Traditional)** - 10 reviews

**Analysis Details:**
- Total users who registered in 2016: 168
- Total reviews written by these users since 2016: 150
- These reviews were distributed across various business categories as shown above

The "Restaurants" category is the clear leader with 22 reviews, followed by "American (New)" cuisine with 16 reviews. The "Food" category (14 reviews) and "Local Services" (11 reviews) also received significant attention from this user cohort, along with "American (Traditional)" (10 reviews)."}

message.reasoning:
The user is asking: "Among users who registered on Yelp in 2016, which 5 business categories have received the most total reviews from those users since 2016?"

Let me trace through my analysis:

1. I first identified users who registered in 2016 by querying the user table with yelping_since containing '2016'. I found 168 such users.

2. I then needed to find all reviews written by these users. I loaded all reviews and filtered them to only include reviews where the user_id matched the 2016 users.

3. For each review, I needed to identify the business category. The challenge was that the business collection in MongoDB has descriptions like "Located at 6901 Phelps Rd in Goleta, CA, this facility offers a range of services in Education, Elementary Schools, Child Care & Day Care, Local Services, Preschools, and Montessori Schools."

4. I created a mapping between business references (businessref_X) and business IDs (businessid_X) by extracting the numeric part.

5. For each review by a 2016 user, I:
   - Looked up the business using the business_ref
   - Extracted the description
   - Parsed the description to find business categories (looking for patterns like "in Category1, Category2, and Category3" or "including Category1, Category2")

6. I counted reviews per category and found the top 5:
   - Restaurants: 22 reviews
   - American (New): 16 reviews
   - Food: 14 reviews
   - Local Services: 11 reviews
   - American (Traditional): 10 reviews

7. Total reviews processed from 2016 users: 150

The analysis shows that these 168 users who joined in 2016 have written 150 reviews total since 2016 (including after 2016), with "Restaurants" being the most reviewed category.

Let me provide the final answer clearly.

So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

- return_answer:

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Among users who registered on Yelp in 2016, which 5 business categories have received the most total reviews from those users since 2016?

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. businessinfo_database
   - This database is stored in a MongoDB database and contains metadata and user check-in records for Yelp businesses. It captures business attributes, service descriptions, operating hours, and user visit timestamps.
   - This database consists of two collections:
     - business
       - This collection contains metadata for businesses listed on Yelp, including names, identifiers, operational status, attributes, business hours, and service/location descriptions.
       - Fields:
         - _id
         - business_id (str): Unique business identifier
         - name (str): Business name
         - review_count (int): Total number of reviews
         - is_open (int): Whether business is currently open (1=open, 0=closed)
         - attributes (dict or null): Business attributes (parking, WiFi, etc.)
         - hours (dict or null): Operating hours
         - description (str): Business description including location information

     - checkin
       - This collection records the check-in behavior of Yelp users at various businesses, capturing the timestamped log of user presence.
       - Fields:
         - _id
         - business_id (str): Business identifier linking to business collection
         - date (list of str): List of check-in timestamps

2. user_database
   - This database is stored in a DuckDB database and contains user profiles and user-generated content from Yelp, including written reviews, tips, and engagement statistics such as review counts, votes, and elite status.
   - This database consists of three tables:
     - review
       - This table contains user-generated reviews of businesses on Yelp, including rating scores, review content, and metadata such as user and business reference identifiers.
       - Fields:
         - review_id (str): Unique review identifier
         - user_id (str or null): User identifier
         - business_ref (str): Business identifier linking to the business collection
         - rating (int): Rating score (1-5)
         - useful (int): Useful votes received
         - funny (int): Funny votes received
         - cool (int): Cool votes received
         - text (str): Review text content
         - date (str): Review date

     - tip
       - This table contains short user tips about Yelp businesses, usually concise suggestions or comments, along with optional compliments and timestamps.
       - Fields:
         - user_id (str or null): User identifier
         - business_ref (str): Business identifier linking to the business collection
         - text (str): Tip text content
         - date (str): Tip date
         - compliment_count (int): Number of compliments received

     - user
       - This table contains user profile information from Yelp, including activity statistics and membership history.
       - Fields:
         - user_id (str): Unique user identifier
         - name (str): User name
         - review_count (int): Total reviews written by user
         - yelping_since (str): Registration date
         - useful (int): Total useful votes received
         - funny (int): Total funny votes received
         - cool (int): Total cool votes received
         - elite (str): Elite status years

HINTS:
- The "business_id" field in the business collection corresponds to the "business_ref" fields in both the review table and the tip table. The values differ only by their prefixes: "business_id" uses the prefix `businessid_`, while "business_ref" uses the prefix `businessref_`. For example, `businessid_1` in the business collection corresponds to `businessref_1` in the review and tip tables.
- The datasets contain five tables/collections in total. Carefully identify which tables or collections and which fields contain the information required to answer the query.
- The business collection's "description" field includes location information if needed.
- The business collection's "attributes" field includes services information if needed.

print("__RESULT__:")

Below is what I said to him on the phone, but for some reason this is how he wants to handle the situation publicly. I also wasn't going to include this update, but I don't want anyone else to experience what we went through. In the days after returning from grooming, my puppy was itching nonstop. We decided to give her a bath even though she just got "groomed", and when her fur was wet we saw that she was COVERED in fleas. This is a 4 month old puppy that has been inside 100% of the time and not around any other dogs, and somehow she returns from this grooming session with fleas??? After washing everything in our house, using medicated shampoo, and combing through her fur, we finally were able to get rid of the infestation. Where was she when the groomer was on his many smoke breaks? In a dirty crate? I'm sure they didn't expect me to be in my car out front watching the whole time. Beware!!

******

I struggled with writing this review because this is a small business and the owner seems very nice. This is review is about James, the groomer, in particular. My puppy visited for the first time today - she's 4 months old and this is her second grooming session. Her first groomer is 45 min away, and Pit Stop HQ was a closer option. 

When I dropped my puppy off, James seemed like he couldn't be bothered listening to what I wanted for the cut. I wrestled with leaving her with him at all, because my gut told me he wasn't going to handle her with care. They said it would be about an hour to an hour and a half, so I decided to wait out front in my car, especially given my uneasiness. Within 5 minutes, James was outside on a smoke break. Ok, fine...everyone needs breaks. Over an hour later, I watched him come out again for another smoke break, then he disappeared down the block. I walked into the shop wondering where my dog was while he's taking a stroll...5 minutes later he comes back and says another hour! I don't think he had even started on my dog the whole time I was waiting in my car outside. 

Despite my annoyance, I told him to take his time so it was done right. Ten minutes later I got a call saying she was all done. I didn't get a chance to look that closely until I got in the car, but the cut was SO choppy and she looked absolutely terrible. He definitely rushed and didn't care at all about how she looked. I even told him at drop off that we have family portraits scheduled for tomorrow, and now I have to cancel the session. I was really hoping to get pictures done while she was still a small puppy, but I have to wait until her hair grows out again. I don't even care about the $60 I spent - I could just tell my puppy wasn't treated with respect or dignity from my interactions with James.

I spent an hour bribing her with treats when I got home to fix the uneven cut. The picture below is after struggling to fix it - it was much worse before. The picture with the yellow bow is her last grooming session with someone else. I never write bad reviews, especially because it can hurt small businesses...but this experience was too awful not to share. She is also the only reason I put up with the atrocious customer service at this location and unfair charges for so long. I have been having issues with the app not booking me in classes, showing me as not in classes I am booked in, and not cancelling classes I cancelled in time. This has resulted in several disputable charges on my account. Each time I called I was told there were no other reported issues with the app and I must be mistaken. And that if there was an issue with the app, they are just a franchise and the app is owned and ran by orange theory not the franchise location. This went on for months and to be perfectly honest I thought maybe I was mistaken. Last week I was booked in Kelly's 10 am Saturday class and I screenshotted it in the app as booked and screenshotted it in my calendar (as this is also done via the app). I was on the wait list and often check my wait list number throughout the week. On Friday when I went to see if I had been bumped up, I was no longer booked in the class. When I called to ask them about the app, she told me I was still on the wait list as number 2. At this point I flipped out because had I not called I would have just found another workout for that morning assuming I was again mistaken AGAIN and thus "no showed" thinking I wasn't booked in the class when I was. Thus, resulting in a charge for a class I "no showed", when in reality the app is not providing accurate info but I am making decisions as though it is. Thank god I screenshotted it or else they could have continued dodging me and saying I am mistaken. I asked Emily if I could please have a number to speak with someone at orange theory about the app issues. After being on hold for 10 minutes she came back and said that the manager would call me. It has been almost a week and no call. I am cancelling my membership today, but if you want good customer service, accountability, and you don't want to be robbed blind, I suggest you use a different orange theory location. I am genuinely upset about this because like I said, Kelly is worth the bullshit because she is such an amazing trainer. They are lucky to have her and most people probably put up with their bullshit because of her. I will probably pay the higher "drop in fee" to get my Kelly fix, but I won't let them have access to any of my cards via a membership. They will take your money with zero remorse as to whether you or their app caused the issue. function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Load all data
users_2016_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:20']
businesses_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:30']

with open(users_2016_file, 'r') as f:
    users_2016 = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

with open(businesses_file, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Extract user IDs for 2016
user_ids_2016 = set(user['user_id'] for user in users_2016)
print(f"Total users from 2016: {len(user_ids_2016)}")

# Filter reviews by these users
reviews_by_2016_users = [
    review for review in reviews 
    if review['user_id'] in user_ids_2016
]
print(f"Total reviews by 2016 users: {len(reviews_by_2016_users)}")

# Create business ID mapping (businessref_X -> businessid_X)
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']  # e.g., businessid_49
    # Extract the number part
    num = business_id.split('_')[1]
    business_ref = f"businessref_{num}"
    business_map[business_ref] = business

print(f"Total businesses: {len(business_map)}")

# Extract categories and count reviews
category_review_count = Counter()
review_per_business = {}

for review in reviews_by_2016_users:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if business_ref in business_map:
        business = business_map[business_ref]
        description = business.get('description', '')
        
        # Extract categories from description
        # Remove location prefix if present
        desc_clean = re.sub(r'^Located at [^,]+, [^,]+, [^,]+, ', '', description)
        
        # Extract categories (look for patterns like "in Category1, Category2, and Category3" or "in Category1, Category2, Category3")
        categories = []
        
        # Try different patterns
        patterns = [
            r'in ([^\.]+)',  # "in Category1, Category2, and Category3"
            r'including ([^\.]+)',  # "including Category1, Category2, and Category3"
            r'offers ([^\.]+)',  # "offers Category1, Category2, and Category3"
        ]
        
        for pattern in patterns:
            match = re.search(pattern, desc_clean)
            if match:
                cats_text = match.group(1)
                # Split by commas and "and"
                cats = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', cats_text)
                categories = [cat.strip() for cat in cats if cat.strip()]
                break
        
        # If no pattern matched, try splitting the whole description
        if not categories:
            # For descriptions like "Education, Elementary Schools, Child Care..."
            cats = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', desc_clean)
            categories = [cat.strip() for cat in cats if cat.strip()]
        
        # Count reviews per category
        for category in categories:
            category_review_count[category] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5_categories = category_review_count.most_common(5)

print("\nTop 5 business categories by total reviews from 2016 users:")
for i, (category, count) in enumerate(top_5_categories, 1):
    print(f"{i}. {category}: {count} reviews")

result = {
    "top_5_categories": [
        {"category": cat, "review_count": count}
        for cat, count in top_5_categories
    ],
    "total_reviews_analyzed": len(reviews_by_2016_users)
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

{"code": "import json
from collections import Counter
import re

# Load the data from stored files
print("Loading data from files...")

# Load 2016 users (var_functions.query_db:12)
print("Loading 2016 users...")
users_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(users_file, 'r') as f:
    users_2016 = json.load(f)
user_ids_2016 = set(user['user_id'] for user in users_2016)
print(f"Found {len(user_ids_2016)} users from 2016")

# Load all reviews (var_functions.query_db:36)
print("Loading all reviews...")
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    all_reviews = json.load(f)
print(f"Total reviews loaded: {len(all_reviews)}")

# Filter reviews by 2016 users
reviews_by_2016_users = [
    review for review in all_reviews 
    if review['user_id'] in user_ids_2016
]
print(f"Reviews by 2016 users: {len(reviews_by_2016_users)}")

# Create business reference map (businessref_X -> business object)
print("Loading businesses...")
businesses_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:30']
with open(businesses_file, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']  # e.g., businessid_49
    num = business_id.split('_')[1]
    business_ref = f"businessref_{num}"
    business_map[business_ref] = business

print(f"Mapped {len(business_map)} businesses")

# Process reviews and extract categories
category_counter = Counter()
unmatched_businesses = set()

print("Processing reviews and extracting categories...")
for review in reviews_by_2016_users:
    business_ref = review.get('business_ref')
    if not business_ref:
        continue
        
    if business_ref not in business_map:
        unmatched_businesses.add(business_ref)
        continue
    
    business = business_map[business_ref]
    description = business.get('description', '')
    
    # Extract categories from description
    categories = []
    desc_clean = re.sub(r'^Located at [^,]+, [^,]+, [^,]+, ', '', description)
    
    # Try to extract categories after keywords like "in", "including", "offers"
    patterns = [
        r'in ([^\.]+)',
        r'including ([^\.]+)',
        r'offers ([^\.]+)',
        r'provides ([^\.]+)',
        r'offering ([^\.]+)'
    ]
    
    found_categories = False
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc_clean)
        if match:
            cats_text = match.group(1)
            # Split by comma and "and"
            cats = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', cats_text)
            categories = [cat.strip() for cat in cats if cat.strip()]
            if categories:
                found_categories = True
                break
    
    # If no pattern matched, try the entire cleaned description
    if not found_categories:
        cats = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', desc_clean)
        categories = [cat.strip() for cat in cats if cat.strip()]
    
    # Count reviews for each category
    for category in categories:
        category_counter[category] += 1

print(f"Unmatched business references: {len(unmatched_businesses)}")

# Get top 5 categories
print("\nTop 5 categories by review count:")
top_5 = category_counter.most_common(10)  # Get more to see patterns
for i, (cat, count) in enumerate(top_5[:5], 1):
    print(f"{i}. {cat}: {count} reviews")

result = {
    "top_5_categories": [
        {"category": cat, "review_count": count}
        for cat, count in top_5[:5]
    ],
    "total_reviews_analyzed": len(reviews_by_2016_users),
    "total_categories_found": len(category_counter)
}

print("\n__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

{"code": "import json
from collections import Counter
import re

# Load 2016 users
users_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(users_data, 'r') as f:
    users_2016 = json.load(f)

user_ids_2016 = set(user['user_id'] for user in users_2016)
print(f"Loaded {len(user_ids_2016)} users from 2016")

# Load all reviews
reviews_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
with open(reviews_data, 'r') as f:
    all_reviews = json.load(f)

print(f"Loaded {len(all_reviews)} total reviews")

# Filter reviews by 2016 users
reviews_by_2016_users = [r for r in all_reviews if r.get('user_id') in user_ids_2016]
print(f"Found {len(reviews_by_2016_users)} reviews by 2016 users")

# Load businesses
businesses_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:30']
with open(businesses_data, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Create mapping from business_ref to business
business_map = {}
for b in businesses:
    business_id = b['business_id']
    num = business_id.split('_')[1]
    business_ref = f"businessref_{num}"
    business_map[business_ref] = b

print(f"Mapped {len(business_map)} businesses")

# Process categories
category_counter = Counter()
unmatched = 0

for review in reviews_by_2016_users:
    business_ref = review.get('business_ref')
    if not business_ref or business_ref not in business_map:
        unmatched += 1
        continue
    
    description = business_map[business_ref].get('description', '')
    
    # Clean description
    desc_clean = re.sub(r'^Located at [^,]+, [^,]+, [^,]+, ', '', description)
    desc_clean = re.sub(r'^Located at [^,]+, [^,]+, [^\.]+\.?\s*', '', desc_clean)
    
    # Extract categories
    categories = []
    patterns = [r'in ([^\.]+)', r'including ([^\.]+)', r'offers ([^\.]+)', r'category of [^']*'([^\']+)'[^']*']
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc_clean, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            cats_text = match.group(1)
            # Split by comma and "and"
            cats = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', cats_text)
            categories = [c.strip() for c in cats if c.strip()]
            break
    
    # Fallback: split by commas
    if not categories:
        cats = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', desc_clean)
        categories = [c.strip() for c in cats if c.strip() and len(c.strip()) < 100]
    
    # Count reviews per category
    for category in categories:
        category_counter[category] += 1

print(f"Unmatched business refs: {unmatched}")
print(f"Total unique categories: {len(category_counter)}")

# Get top 5
top_5 = category_counter.most_common(5)
print("\nTop 5 categories:")
for i, (cat, count) in enumerate(top_5, 1):
    print(f"{i}. {cat}: {count} reviews")

result = {
    "top_5_categories": [(cat, count) for cat, count in top_5],
    "total_reviews_analyzed": len(reviews_by_2016_users),
    "unmatched_businesses": unmatched
}

print("\n__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))"}

{"code": "import json
from collections import Counter
import re

# Load 2016 users
with open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:12'], 'r') as f:
    users_2016 = json.load(f)
user_ids_2016 = set(user['user_id'] for user in users_2016)

# Load all reviews
with open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:36'], 'r') as f:
    all_reviews = json.load(f)

# Filter reviews by 2016 users
reviews_2016 = [r for r in all_reviews if r.get('user_id') in user_ids_2016]

# Load businesses and create mapping
with open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:30'], 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

business_map = {}
for b in businesses:
    num = b['business_id'].split('_')[1]
    business_map[f"businessref_{num}"] = b

# Count reviews per category
category_counts = Counter()
processed = 0

for review in reviews_2016:
    business_ref = review.get('business_ref')
    if business_ref and business_ref in business_map:
        desc = business_map[business_ref].get('description', '')
        # Clean description
        desc_clean = re.sub(r'^Located at [^,]+, [^,]+, [^,]+, ', '', desc)
        desc_clean = re.sub(r'^Located at [^\.]+\.\s*', '', desc_clean)
        
        # Extract categories - look for text after "in" or "including"
        categories = []
        for pattern in [r'in ([^\.]+)', r'including ([^\.]+)']:
            match = re.search(pattern, desc_clean)
            if match:
                cats = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s+', match.group(1))
                categories = [c.strip() for c in cats if c.strip()]
                break
        
        # Count each category
        for cat in categories:
            category_counts[cat] += 1
        processed += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5 = category_counts.most_common(5)

result = {
    'top_5_categories': [(cat, count) for cat, count in top_5],
    'total_reviews_processed': processed,
    'total_2016_users': len(user_ids_2016)
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))"}
